content:
# Genesis Chapter 33 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 19r–19v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_033_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_033_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 30–31 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0034 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0035

---

**Manuscript Confirmation:** The Leningrad Codex images (Folios 19r–19v, West Semitic Research Project, Bruce E. Zuckerman) have been read directly by all three council translators. Folio 19r (GEN_033_page_001.jpg) carries the conclusion of Genesis 32 (the sinew prohibition) and then the full opening of Genesis 33 — the family arrangement (vv. 1–2), Jacob's sevenfold prostration (v. 3), the reconciliation (v. 4), the family introductions (vv. 5–7), the gift dialogue (vv. 8–11), and Esau's offer of escort (vv. 12–15). The three-column Sephardic square script with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks is legible throughout. The *puncta extraordinaria* above וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ in v. 4 are confirmed by all three translators. Folio 19v (GEN_033_page_002.jpg) carries Genesis 33:16–20 — Esau's return to Seir (v. 16), Jacob's journey to Succoth (v. 17), the arrival at Shechem (v. 18), the land purchase (v. 19), and the altar inscription El-Elohe-Israel (v. 20) — before continuing into Genesis 34. The divine name in Genesis 33 is אֱלֹהִים (*Elohim*) throughout; יְהוָה (YHWH) does not appear in this chapter. Codex Vaticanus pages 30–31 carry the Greek LXX in continuous uncial, with the chapter heading CAP. XXXVII on page 30 and CAP. XXXVIII beginning on page 31. No Dead Sea Scrolls fragments of Genesis 33 are extant; the Leningrad Codex and Codex Vaticanus are the principal manuscript witnesses examined for this chapter.

---

*Jacob Sees Esau Coming — He Arranges His Family (vv. 1–3)*

**33:1** And Jacob lifted up his eyes and saw — and behold, Esau was coming, and with him four hundred men. So he divided the children among Leah and Rachel and the two female servants.

**33:2** He placed the female servants and their children first, and Leah and her children after them, and Rachel and Joseph last of all.

**33:3** Then he himself went on ahead of them, and bowed to the ground seven times, until he came near to his brother.

---

*Esau Runs to Meet Jacob — The Reconciliation (v. 4)*

**33:4** But Esau ran to meet him and embraced him, and fell on his neck and kissed him ˙˙˙˙˙˙˙ — and they wept.

---

*Jacob Introduces His Family to Esau (vv. 5–7)*

**33:5** Then Esau lifted up his eyes and saw the women and the children, and said, "Who are these with you?" Jacob said, "The children whom God has graciously given your servant."

**33:6** Then the female servants drew near, they and their children, and they bowed down.

**33:7** Leah also drew near with her children, and they bowed down. And afterward Joseph and Rachel drew near, and they bowed down.

---

*The Question of the Gift-Caravan (vv. 8–11)*

**33:8** Esau said, "What do you mean by all this company that I met?" Jacob said, "To find favor in the eyes of my lord."

**33:9** But Esau said, "I have more than enough, my brother — keep what you have for yourself."

**33:10** Jacob said, "No, please — if I have found favor in your eyes, then accept my gift from my hand. For I have seen your face as one sees the face of God, and you have received me with favor.

**33:11** Please accept my blessing that has been brought to you, for God has been gracious to me, and because I have everything." And he urged him, and he took it.

---

*Esau Offers an Escort — Jacob Declines (vv. 12–15)*

**33:12** Then Esau said, "Let us journey on our way, and I will go before you."

**33:13** But Jacob said to him, "My lord knows that the children are tender, and the nursing flocks and herds are a care to me. If they are driven hard for even one day, all the flocks will die.

**33:14** Let my lord pass on ahead of his servant. I myself will lead along gently at the pace of the livestock that are before me and at the pace of the children, until I come to my lord at Seir."

**33:15** Esau said, "Let me then leave some of my people with you." But Jacob said, "Why should this be? Let me find favor in the eyes of my lord."

---

*The Parting of Ways — Esau Returns to Seir (v. 16)*

**33:16** So Esau returned that day on his way to Seir.

---

*Jacob Journeys to Succoth (v. 17)*

**33:17** And Jacob traveled to Succoth, and built himself a house and made booths for his livestock. Therefore the name of the place is called Succoth.

---

*Jacob Arrives at Shechem — Buys Land — Erects an Altar (vv. 18–20)*

**33:18** And Jacob came safely to the city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan, when he came from Paddan-aram, and he camped before the city.

**33:19** And he bought the piece of land where he had pitched his tent from the sons of Hamor, the father of Shechem, for a hundred *qesitah*.

**33:20** And he set up there an altar and called it El-Elohe-Israel.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### The Architecture of Genesis 33: Reconciliation, Parting, Arrival

All three council translators identified the same three-movement structure. PNEUMA's formulation captures it most sharply: "Genesis 32 is the night. Genesis 33 is the morning." The chapter moves from the charged meeting and reconciliation (vv. 1–11), through the careful parting (vv. 12–16), to Jacob's arrival and settlement at Shechem (vv. 17–20). It is framed at each end by the geography of the two brothers: Esau comes from Seir (vv. 1, 16) and Jacob ends by building an altar using his new name — Israel — for the first time in a divine title (v. 20). LOGOS provides the sharpest structural theology: the chapter closes in mirror-image reversal of the betrayal in Genesis 27–28. Jacob fled eastward, deceiving his father; he returns from the east, bowing seven times before his brother. The man who took arrives bearing gifts and calling them blessings.

The council also adopts PNEUMA's observation regarding v. 4 and Luke 15: the structural parallel between Esau running to embrace Jacob and the father running to embrace the prodigal son is exact — father/brother runs, falls on the neck, kisses — and was almost certainly drawn consciously from this passage.

---

### On 33:1 — "Lifted up his eyes and saw" (*wayyissa' einav wayyar'*)

All three translators are agreed: the phrase נָשָׂא עֵינַיִם (*nasa' einayim*, "to lift up the eyes") is a recurring Genesis idiom for a character turning attention to something unexpected, and must not be reduced to "he looked up." LOGOS articulates the governing principle explicitly — "if the source says 'he lifted his eyes,' do not write 'he looked up'" — and all three translators follow it. The council adopts the literal rendering throughout. EMET and LOGOS both note the parallel with Genesis 22:4 and 22:13 (Abraham at Moriah); PNEUMA does not explicitly invoke these parallels but translates identically. The LXX Vaticanus reads *anablepsas de Iakōb tois ophthalmois eiden* — "and Jacob, having looked up with his eyes, saw" — a participial construction semantically equivalent to the MT.

---

### On 33:1–2 — The Family Arrangement

There is complete agreement across all three translators on the content and order of the arrangement. EMET provides the sharpest observation — adopted by the council — on the moral logic of the ordering: Jacob positions those he loves least at the front, where danger would strike first, and those he loves most (Rachel, Joseph) at the rear. This is one of the most coldly calculated acts in the Jacob narrative, and the text reports it without commentary. PNEUMA frames it aptly: "the last act of the calculating Jacob; what happens next undoes all the calculation in a moment." The council adopts this as its standing characterization of the passage.

---

### On 33:3 — Seven Prostrations

All three translators are agreed on the rendering. The sevenfold prostration before Esau is noted by EMET with the most detailed parallel: the Amarna letters (14th century BCE) record the same seven-times-and-seven-times formula of diplomatic submission from Canaanite vassals to the Egyptian Pharaoh. Jacob — destined by Isaac's blessing to be lord over his brother (27:29) — performs the vassal's prostration before the one he supplanted. The structural irony requires no interpretive comment in the translation text; it stands in the sequence of the narrative.

---

### On 33:4 — The Puncta Extraordinaria on *vayyishshaqehu* (וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ) ⚠️

**Full discussion in Disagreement Log below.**

The council adopts EMET's practice of marking the puncta in the translation text itself, using the symbol ˙˙˙˙˙˙˙ after "kissed him." This preserves the scribal signal as a visible feature of the text for the reader, without adjudicating between the rabbinic interpretations. The LXX Vaticanus has no equivalent mark; it reads the reconciliation without hesitation (LOGOS notes *katephilesen* — "kissed him fervently/tenderly"; PNEUMA notes *ephilesen* — "kissed him"). The council renders the kiss plainly — "kissed him" — and reserves the full discussion to the notes.

---

### On 33:5 — "God has graciously given" (*channan Elohim*)

All three translators render this as "graciously given" or "graciously given to" — the verb חָנַן (*chanan*, to be gracious, to give as a gift of grace) in the perfect with God as subject. EMET's note is adopted: the same word *chen* (grace, favor) that Jacob has been seeking from Esau throughout his approach now appears in Jacob's description of his children — they are themselves an embodiment of the grace (*chen*) that Jacob sought. LOGOS correctly notes the contrast with the LXX, which renders *ēleēsen ho Theos* — "God had mercy" (*eleeō*) — a slight semantic shift from grace-as-gift toward compassionate mercy.

---

### On 33:9 — "More than enough" (*yesh-li rav*) and the Esau/Jacob Contrast

LOGOS provides the definitive note on the contrast between Esau's "I have more than enough" (*yesh-li rav*, "I have much") and Jacob's "I have everything" (*yesh-li khol*, v. 11). The two Hebrew expressions are distinct: Esau's *rav* (much, abundant) is quantitative; Jacob's *khol* (all, everything) is totalizing. The council preserves this distinction: Esau says "I have more than enough" (v. 9); Jacob says "I have everything" (v. 11). PNEUMA's rendering of Esau's reply as "I already have more than enough, brother" captures both the substance and the affectionate register (*achi*, "my brother") appropriately. The council adopts this warmth.

---

### On 33:10 — "As one sees the face of God" — The Peniel Echo ⚠️

All three translators identify the theological weight of this verse and preserve the face-seeing comparison without softening. EMET's note is the fullest: at Peniel, Jacob declared "I have seen God face to face" (32:30); now he says seeing Esau's welcoming face is like seeing the face of God. This is not flattery — it is a structural theological statement: Jacob passed through the gauntlet of divine encounter and human encounter both, and was accepted at both. PNEUMA's formulation is adopted as the council's summary: "He crossed the Jabbok terrified of Esau; he crossed the Jabbok and met God; and now, on the other side, Esau's face is God's face — a face Jacob expected to be set against him, that was instead, impossibly, full of welcome." The LXX Vaticanus preserves the simile exactly. The council renders the full comparison — "as one sees the face of God" — without reduction.

---

### On 33:11 — *birkhati* ("my blessing") ⚠️

**Full discussion in Disagreement Log below.**

The council renders v. 11 as "my blessing" following EMET and the LXX (*eulogias mou*), rather than "my gift" (LOGOS). The word בִּרְכָתִי (*birkhati*, "my blessing") is the same root-word that governed the blessing Jacob stole in Genesis 27. PNEUMA's interpretation is adopted by the council: "Jacob is returning — as much as he can, in the form of wealth — the blessing he stole. The word *birkhati* is loaded with everything that has happened between these two men since the day at Isaac's bedside." Rendering this as "my gift" (as LOGOS does for contextual intelligibility) dissolves the deliberate verbal echo. The council retains "my blessing."

---

### On 33:13–14 — Jacob's Pastoral Refusal

The council follows LOGOS on the phrase לְרֶגֶל (*le-regel*, "at the foot/pace of") — rendering "at the pace of the livestock" and "at the pace of the children." EMET's more awkward literal rendering ("to the foot of the work") is honest to the Hebrew idiom but too opaque in English. PNEUMA's paraphrase ("following whatever the animals and the children can manage") is vivid but slightly interpretive. LOGOS's "at the pace of" is the optimal balance.

The council also notes the significant LXX Vaticanus variant here: where the MT has Jacob say *le'atti* — "gently, slowly" — the LXX reads *en tachei* — "quickly." This is the most notable LXX divergence in the chapter, apparently reversing the sense of the MT. The council follows the MT throughout; the LXX variant is documented for completeness.

---

### On 33:16 — The Promise to Come to Seir — Never Fulfilled

All three translators observe that Jacob's promise in v. 14 — "until I come to my lord at Seir" — is never fulfilled in the narrative. Esau departs for Seir; Jacob travels to Succoth and Shechem. EMET's formulation is adopted: "the separation is the end of the story. The two brothers have reconciled — and parted." PNEUMA's more nuanced reading is also adopted: the reconciliation is real, but the men are not the same people they were. The wound has healed; the brothers do not travel together. The text does not call Jacob a liar here, and the council declines to adjudicate.

---

### On 33:17 — Succoth (*Sukkot*, סֻכּוֹת)

All three translators are agreed on the rendering: "Succoth" as the place name, "booths" as the common noun, preserving the etymological play. LOGOS's note is adopted: the word *sukkah* (booth/shelter) is the same root as the Festival of Booths (*Chag ha-Sukkot*, Leviticus 23), drawing a line from Jacob's pastoral shelters to the liturgical life of Israel. The LXX Vaticanus renders *Sokkōth* and *skēnas* (tabernacles/tents) — consistent with the MT's wordplay. The council renders "booths" consistently with the Festival of Booths connection in view.

---

### On 33:18 — *shalem* — "Safely" or "Shalem" (Salem)? ⚠️

**Full discussion in Disagreement Log below.**

The council adopts the adverbial reading — "safely" — following LOGOS and PNEUMA (and the majority of modern formal translations). The word שָׁלֵם (*shalem*) most probably modifies Jacob's manner of arrival: he came *whole, unharmed, at peace*. This reading provides theological closure to Jacob's Bethel vow (28:21, *we-shavti be-shalom*, "if I return in peace to my father's house"). PNEUMA's formulation is the council's preferred statement: "The man who left Canaan with a walking staff and nothing else has come back *shalem* — complete, at peace, whole." The LXX Vaticanus reads *eis Salēm polin Sikimōn* ("into Shalem, a city of the Shechemites"), treating it as a proper noun; this alternative reading is preserved in the Disagreement Log.

---

### On 33:19 — *qesitah* (קְשִׂיטָה) ⚠️

**Full discussion in Disagreement Log below.**

The council transliterates *qesitah* in the translation text and preserves "a hundred" exactly, following EMET's principle that an untranslatable term should be reported as it stands rather than domesticated into an approximate equivalent. The word appears only here, in Job 42:11, and in Joshua 24:32 (the latter explicitly citing this same purchase). LOGOS renders "a hundred pieces of money" (traditional English convention); PNEUMA renders "a hundred pieces of silver" (inferential). The LXX Vaticanus renders *hekaton amnōn* ("a hundred lambs") — a fascinating ancient interpretation that may preserve a genuine tradition about the unit's value in livestock, but it is not the MT's reading. The council retains the technical term *qesitah* in transliteration, as its untranslatability is itself significant data about the text's antiquity.

---

### On 33:20 — El-Elohe-Israel (אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל) ⚠️

**Full discussion in Disagreement Log below.**

The council retains "El-Elohe-Israel" as a proper name in the translation text, following LOGOS and EMET, rather than translating it inline (PNEUMA renders it as "El-Elohe-Israel — God, the God of Israel," appending a parenthetical gloss). The altar name's meaning is transparent from the elements: *El* (God) + *Elohei Yisra'el* (God of Israel). EMET's note on the formula is adopted by the council: this is the first time Jacob uses the name Israel — given at Peniel (32:28) — in a theological self-designation. The altar is simultaneously an act of worship, a claim of sacred territory at Shechem (echoing Abraham's first altar in Canaan, 12:6–7), and a formal adoption of the covenant name. LOGOS provides the crucial additional observation: the altar name echoes the confessor-pattern of patriarchal religion — "the God of Abraham," "the God of Isaac," now "the God of Israel" — declaring that the God Jacob wrestled with is specifically and personally his God. The council adopts both observations. The LXX Vaticanus reads *Theos ho theos Israēl* ("God, the God of Israel") — a translation rather than a transliteration, and it omits the initial *El* that appears in the MT's אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל. The MT's doubled title — *El-Elohe-Israel* — carries greater emphatic weight, and the council follows the MT.

---

### Summary of SOPHIA's Consensus Decisions

| Verse | Decision | From |
|-------|----------|------|
| 33:1 | "And Jacob lifted up his eyes and saw" — idiom preserved in full | All three (unanimous) |
| 33:4 | "kissed him ˙˙˙˙˙˙˙" — puncta marked visually, reconciliation rendered plainly | EMET (puncta marking) |
| 33:9 | "more than enough" (*rav*) — quantitative, warm | PNEUMA |
| 33:10 | "as one sees the face of God" — Peniel echo fully preserved | All three (unanimous) |
| 33:11 | "my blessing" (*birkhati*) — verbal echo of Genesis 27 preserved | EMET / PNEUMA (over LOGOS "my gift") |
| 33:14 | "at the pace of the livestock / at the pace of the children" | LOGOS |
| 33:17 | "Succoth" / "booths" — etymological play preserved | All three (unanimous) |
| 33:18 | "safely" — adverbial reading of *shalem* | LOGOS / PNEUMA (over EMET's transliteration) |
| 33:19 | "*qesitah*" — transliterated, untranslated | EMET |
| 33:20 | "El-Elohe-Israel" — proper name retained | EMET / LOGOS |

---

## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### ⚠️ 33:4 — The Puncta Extraordinaria on *vayyishshaqehu* (וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ)

**Issue:** The word וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ (*vayyishshaqehu*, "and he kissed him") bears raised dots above each letter in the Masoretic Text — *puncta extraordinaria* (*neqüddot*), one of fifteen such scribal annotations in the Torah. Confirmed by all three translators on the Leningrad Codex image. The rabbinic tradition offers multiple interpretations: (1) the kiss was not genuine — Esau tried to bite (*nashakh*) rather than kiss (*nashaq*) Jacob, and the dots mark the word as suspect; (2) the kiss was genuine and the dots signal that this is *extraordinary* — that Esau truly embraced the brother who stole his blessing; (3) the dots represent an ancient scribal uncertainty about whether the word was original to the text. The LXX Vaticanus has no equivalent; it reads the kiss without hesitation.

**EMET's position:** Marks the puncta in the translation with ˙˙˙˙˙˙˙ following "and he kissed him." Declines to adjudicate among the three rabbinic readings. Reports the textual signal as it stands.

**LOGOS's position:** Renders the kiss plainly ("kissed him") and notes the puncta in the translation notes, without marking them in the body text. Notes that the LXX *katephilesen auton* ("kissed him fervently") resolves the ambiguity toward the sincere-kiss reading.

**PNEUMA's position:** Renders plainly ("kissed him") and devotes an extended note to the puncta. Reads the scene as genuine — "the tears are there, the five verbs move too fast for theater" — while acknowledging the dots mark the word as *extraordinary*, because the kiss of Esau is indeed extraordinary.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts EMET's practice of visibly marking the puncta in the translation text (˙˙˙˙˙˙˙ after "kissed him"). The scribal signal is part of the text as it has been transmitted; to render the verse without any indication of it would erase a feature of the manuscript that all three council translators observed and that the Masoretes preserved with evident care. However, the council follows PNEUMA's reading of the scene as genuine reconciliation: the five rapid verbs (ran, embraced, fell on his neck, kissed, wept) move without pause; the tears — both men weeping together — are not the behavior of an ambush. The dots mark the kiss as *extraordinary*, not necessarily as suspect. The council does not adjudicate the full range of rabbinic interpretations but marks the text as the manuscript marks it.

---

### ⚠️ 33:10 — "As one sees the face of God" — Peniel Echo

**Issue:** Jacob says to Esau: "I have seen your face as one sees the face of God" (כִּרְאֹת פְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים, *kir'ot penei Elohim*). This directly echoes Genesis 32:30 — "I have seen God face to face" (*ra'iti Elohim panim el-panim*). The face-language of the Peniel theophany is now applied to the human encounter with Esau. The question is whether to read this as theological claim, as metaphor, or as rhetorical flattery — and whether the translation should signal any of these readings.

**EMET's position:** Renders literally without interpretation: "I have seen your face as one sees the face of God." Notes the Peniel echo comprehensively but keeps the text unadorned.

**LOGOS's position:** Identical rendering. Notes that the comparison "is not mere flattery; it is a theologically loaded statement" — Jacob has survived seeing God's face and now encounters Esau's welcoming face. Preserves the full comparison.

**PNEUMA's position:** Identical rendering. Provides the most evocative formulation: "He crossed the Jabbok terrified of Esau; he crossed the Jabbok and met God; and now, on the other side, Esau's face is God's face — a face Jacob expected to be set against him, that was instead, impossibly, full of welcome."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three translators agree on the translation text; there is no verbal disagreement. The council logs this entry to record the theological weight that all three translators identify, and to confirm that the Peniel echo must be noted prominently in the consensus notes. PNEUMA's formulation is adopted as the council's theological summary of the verse.

---

### ⚠️ 33:11 — *birkhati* — "My Blessing" vs. "My Gift"

**Issue:** Jacob uses the word בִּרְכָתִי (*birkhati*, "my blessing") for the gift-caravan he presses on Esau. The noun בְּרָכָה (*berakha*) means both "blessing" and, secondarily, "a gift presented as a blessing." The standard word for gift (*minchah*, מִנְחָה) was used throughout Genesis 32 for the approaching gift-caravan. The deliberate switch to *birkhati* here almost certainly echoes the stolen blessing of Genesis 27 (*birkhat yitschaq*, the blessing of Isaac; *birkhatekha*, "your blessing" — Esau's stolen birthright). The LXX Vaticanus renders *tas eulogias mou* ("my blessings"), using the primary theological meaning of *berakha*.

**EMET's position:** Renders "my blessing" (*birkhati*). Notes explicitly that the word is "loaded with everything that has happened between these two men since the day at Isaac's bedside" — Jacob stole Esau's blessing; now he offers his own blessing in return.

**LOGOS's position:** Renders "my gift." Argues that *berakha* in this context "functions contextually as 'gift'" (citing ESV, NASB, NKJV) and that the primary English reader will understand the gift-exchange better with the word "gift." Notes the theological layer in the translation notes but does not let it govern the text.

**PNEUMA's position:** Renders "the blessing I have brought you." Sides with the "blessing" rendering and interprets it as Jacob's return of what was stolen: "Jacob is returning — as much as he can, in the form of wealth — the blessing he stole."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "my blessing" (EMET, PNEUMA) — 2 against 1 — over LOGOS's "my gift." The reasoning: the Leningrad Codex reads בִּרְכָתִי without ambiguity; *birkhati* is not a standard word for gift in Hebrew (that word, *minchah*, was used six times in Genesis 32 for this same caravan); the deliberate switch to *birkhati* at the moment Jacob physically hands over the caravan to Esau is almost certainly not accidental. Rendering it as "my gift" dissolves a verbal echo that the author encoded in the choice of *berakha* over *minchah*. The LXX's *eulogias mou* confirms that the ancient translators read this as "blessing" in the theological sense. LOGOS's concern for English intelligibility is noted; the council adds a brief parenthetical guide in the notes but preserves the word "blessing" in the text itself.

---

### ⚠️ 33:18 — *shalem* — "Safely" (Adverb) vs. "Shalem/Salem" (Proper Noun)

**Issue:** The text reads: וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב שָׁלֵם עִיר שְׁכֶם (*wayyavo Ya'aqov shalem ir Shekhem*). The word שָׁלֵם (*shalem*) is formally ambiguous: it can be read as (a) an adverb/adjective meaning "safely, whole, at peace, in one piece," or (b) a proper noun, the place-name "Shalem" or "Salem," distinct from or identifying a district near Shechem. The Masoretic word stands without a definite article or directional *he* suffix that would resolve the ambiguity definitively. The LXX Vaticanus reads *eis Salēm polin Sikimōn* — "into Shalem, a city of the Shechemites" — treating it as a proper noun. Modern formal translations (NRSV, ESV, NASB, NIV) and most contemporary scholarship prefer the adverbial reading.

**EMET's position:** Transliterates *shalem* in the translation text without resolution ("And Jacob came shalem — a city of Shechem"), marking the ambiguity by preserving both possible readings in the notes. Declines to adjudicate.

**LOGOS's position:** Renders "safely." Argues for the adverbial reading on three grounds: (1) there is no independent geographical attestation of a town named Shalem distinct from Shechem in this region; (2) the word *shalem* connects thematically to Jacob's Bethel vow of return "in peace" (*be-shalom*, 28:21); (3) the narrative logic of Jacob arriving *whole/complete* after the events of Genesis 32–33 provides theological closure to the journey.

**PNEUMA's position:** Renders "safely." Endorses the adverbial reading: "*shalem* — complete, at peace, whole. The journey is over. He is home." Also notes the LXX variant but follows the MT accentuation pointing toward the adverbial sense.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "safely" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) — 2 against 1 — over EMET's transliteration. Three grounds: (1) EMET's practice of leaving *shalem* untranslated in the text body serves the reader less well than choosing one reading with the alternative preserved in the notes, which is what a consensus translation must do; (2) the adverbial reading provides genuine theological closure — the man who left with a staff and a vow to return "in peace" arrives *shalem*; (3) there is no evidence of an otherwise-unknown town named Shalem near Shechem independent of this verse. The LXX's toponym reading (*eis Salēm*) is a significant ancient alternative and is preserved here in the Disagreement Log.

---

### ⚠️ 33:19 — *qesitah* (קְשִׂיטָה) — What Unit of Value?

**Issue:** The price Jacob pays — מֵאָה קְשִׂיטָה (*me'ah qesitah*) — uses a monetary term appearing only three times in the entire Hebrew Bible: here, Job 42:11, and Joshua 24:32. Its etymology and exact value are unknown. Ancient versions diverge significantly: LXX Vaticanus renders *hekaton amnōn* ("a hundred lambs") — treating *qesitah* as a livestock unit; the Vulgate renders *centum agnis* ("a hundred lambs"); the Targums and Peshitta similarly interpret it as a lamb-equivalent. Modern lexicography (BDB, HALOT) identifies *qesitah* as an archaic monetary unit, probably a weight-measure of silver, of unknown value.

**EMET's position:** Transliterates as *qesitah* in the translation text: "for a hundred qesitah." Reports what the text says without domestication. Notes that translating *qesitah* as any known monetary equivalent would misrepresent the text's deliberate use of an archaic, untranslatable term.

**LOGOS's position:** Renders "a hundred pieces of money" — following the traditional English equivalent (KJV convention) and the general sense of a monetary payment. Notes the LXX's lamb-unit reading as a distinct ancient interpretive tradition.

**PNEUMA's position:** Renders "a hundred pieces of silver" — an inferential rendering based on the common ancient payment medium. Preserves the connection to Joshua 24:32 (Joseph's burial site).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts EMET's transliteration — *qesitah* — in the translation text. Rendering it as "pieces of money" (LOGOS) or "pieces of silver" (PNEUMA) injects a precision that the Hebrew text deliberately withholds. The term's opacity is itself a textual feature: it marks the antiquity of the tradition, connecting this purchase to a layer of Israelite legal and economic memory older than the monarchic period. The Joshua 24:32 parallel (which uses the same term for the same field) confirms that *qesitah* is a fixed technical term, not a generic word. "A hundred qesitah" reports what the text says honestly. The LXX's lamb-unit reading (*amnōn*) is the most interesting variant in the chapter — suggesting either a livestock-barter tradition or an ancient attempt to render an opaque term — and is preserved here for completeness.

---

### ⚠️ 33:20 — El-Elohe-Israel — Rendered as Name vs. Translated Inline

**Issue:** The altar inscription אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (*El Elohei Yisra'el*) means "God, the God of Israel." The question is whether to retain it as an untranslated proper name in the translation text ("El-Elohe-Israel"), to translate it inline ("God, the God of Israel"), or to do both.

**EMET's position:** Retains "El-Elohe-Israel" as the altar's proper name in the translation text, with full exegesis in the notes. Notes that the verb וַיִּקְרָא-לוֹ (*wayyiqra'-lo*, "and he called it") treats the phrase as a name, not a description.

**LOGOS's position:** Retains "El-Elohe-Israel" as a proper name. Notes: "the altar name's meaning is immediately transparent from the word-by-word rendering" and the name should be preserved in transliterated form as it is the altar's title. Notes the LXX renders *Theos ho theos Israēl* ("God, the God of Israel") — a translation rather than a transliteration.

**PNEUMA's position:** Retains "El-Elohe-Israel" with a parenthetical gloss: "he called it El-Elohe-Israel — God, the God of Israel." Adds the translation inline as a reader aid.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts EMET and LOGOS — retaining "El-Elohe-Israel" without inline gloss in the translation text, with full explanation in the notes. PNEUMA's parenthetical is well-intentioned but sets a precedent for glossing proper names that the consensus translation generally avoids (cf. Peniel, Mahanaim, Succoth). The name's meaning is given in the council notes, not in the verse itself. The council adopts EMET's observation on the first-use significance: this is the first time Jacob uses the name *Israel* — given at Peniel (32:28) — in a theological title, formally taking ownership of the covenant name. The altar at Shechem echoes Abraham's first altar at that same site (12:6–7), continuing the patriarchal line of sacred-site marking. The LXX's omission of the initial *El* (giving only "God of Israel" rather than "God, the God of Israel") is noted; the MT's doubled title is emphatic and the council follows it.
